title: T2G-Former: Organizing Tabular Features into Relation Graphs Promotes Heterogeneous Feature Interaction
authors: Jiahuan Yan, Jintai Chen, Yixuan Wu, Danny Z. Chen, Jian Wu (Zhejiang University; University of Notre Dame)
tokenization_method: Reuses the FT-Transformer feature tokenizer to embed each column into a per-feature token (numerical: learned linear scaling + bias; categorical: learned embedding lookup + bias). The novel contribution is NOT the tokenizer but a Graph Estimator (GE) that, on top of those tokens, learns a sparse Feature Relation Graph (FR-Graph) controlling which feature tokens interact in an attention-like block.
feature_types_handled: numerical and categorical (static tabular). No temporal/time-series handling. Missing values handled only via standard preprocessing inherited from the FT-Transformer/RTDL benchmark pipeline, not as a modeled token type.
token_granularity: per-feature (one token per table column), identical to FT-Transformer. Plus one extra global 'readout node' token that aggregates salient features across layers (analogous to a CLS token but cross-level).
numerical_strategy: raw scalar passed through a per-feature learned linear embedding + bias (FT-Transformer tokenizer). No piecewise-linear/periodic/quantile numerical embedding (that is the separate Gorishniy 2022 line of work).
categorical_strategy: per-feature learned embedding table + bias (FT-Transformer tokenizer).
high_cardinality_handling: none specific; inherits plain learned embedding tables, so high-cardinality categoricals are not specially treated.
temporal_handling: none — static single-row tabular only; no patching, recurrence, or channel-token mechanism.
scales_to_many_features: Tested on up to 93 features (Helena). The GE scores ALL feature pairs (Gw is fully-connected, O(N^2) pairwise) and then sparsifies the indegree of each feature node via a competitive activation (softmax/entmax/sparsemax). The static topology uses column embeddings of dimension d = 2*ceil(log2 N), giving the topology branch O(N log N) extra parameter complexity (vs O(N^2) for a 'free' learned adjacency, which they show is worse). NET: GE does NOT reduce attention's quadratic cost; it reorganizes/sparsifies WHICH pairwise interactions matter as an inductive bias.
downstream_model: bespoke Transformer-like stack: each 'T2G block' replaces vanilla self-attention scores with the FR-Graph G (G = sigma2(no_self_interaction(A) ⊙ Gw)) times a value projection, with FFN and shortcut/gated fusion; final prediction from the readout node after L layers (default 8-head GE per block).
pretraining_objective: none — fully supervised end-to-end (cross-entropy for classification, MSE for regression). No self-supervised or transfer objective.
key_results: On 12 RTDL-benchmark datasets (15-seed avg, all tuned via Optuna), T2G-Former beats the compared DNNs on 8/12 datasets and is comparable with XGBoost. Standout: California Housing 78.18 acc vs FT-Transformer 70.84 and XGBoost 72.51; Churn 65.57 (XGBoost 68.42 still best); Higgs-Small 73.39 vs FT-T 73.06; Jannis 73.68 vs FT-T 73.24. Best FR-Graph config = symmetric edge weights + asymmetric topology (SwAt). Applying GE to ALL layers beats using it in only one layer or none; removing GE anywhere hurts. Graph-based interaction beats DANet group-based interaction; the column-embedding static topology beats both 'adaptive' (threshold on Gw) and 'free' (learned N-by-N) topologies.
baselines_compared: XGBoost (GBDT representative), MLP, SNN, TabNet, DANet-28, NODE, AutoInt, DCNv2, FT-Transformer. FT-Transformer is the closest architectural baseline (same tokenizer, vanilla attention instead of FR-Graph).
transferable_idea: Decouple the feature tokenizer from the feature-interaction mechanism: keep per-feature tokens, but replace dense self-attention with a LEARNED SPARSE relation graph that combines (a) data-adaptive pairwise edge weights and (b) a dataset-global, instance-independent static topology, then restrict each feature node's indegree via a competitive (sparsemax/entmax) activation. For 70+ heterogeneous features this is a way to impose a learned interaction prior so that only relevant feature pairs interact, rather than letting all N^2 pairs interact uniformly.
limitations: (1) No mechanism for temporal/time-varying features or temporal-static fusion. (2) Gw pairwise scoring is still O(N^2) per layer — does not lower the cost of scaling to many features; the benefit is inductive bias, not efficiency. (3) Static topology A must be frozen after convergence to avoid instability on easy/small/binary tasks (acknowledged training fragility). (4) Single-GE-in-a-high-layer underperforms plain attention on regression — GE helps most in the first layer where feature semantics are clearest. (5) No specific high-cardinality categorical treatment. (6) Largest table tested has 93 features and ~515k rows — not validated at the hundreds-of-features scale.
pitfalls: The 'graph' is not a sparse-compute speedup — Gw is computed fully connected before sparsification, so memory/compute remain quadratic in N (verify before assuming scalability gains). Interpretability claim is suggestive (visualized edges look plausible on California Housing / Churn) but the authors themselves note some learned edges (e.g. Longitude-HouseAge) are 'probably derived from dataset bias' — do not over-read FR-Graph edges as causal feature relations. Reported CA gains are large vs FT-Transformer but XGBoost is still the practical strong baseline; gains are dataset-dependent (only 8/12 wins among DNNs, loses to XGBoost on several).
relevance_to_topic: Directly in-scope as a tabular-transformer whose differentiator is HOW heterogeneous (num+cat) per-feature tokens interact at scale; the FR-Graph offers a learned interaction prior that is the most reusable piece for a 70+-feature setting, but it adds no temporal handling and no compute saving.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/jyansir/t2g-former (PyTorch, official). Uses the RTDL/FT-Transformer data pipeline and Optuna tuning.
